skill.name: routeros-app-yaml
skill.description: Create, review, and troubleshoot RouterOS /app YAML for MikroTik container applications, app stores, ports, placeholders, configs, and REST API workflows.
skill.summary: Guides RouterOS /app YAML creation and review for MikroTik container apps.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and injection indicators, but most are false positives from Markdown documentation, YAML examples, and inline code formatting. No evidence found of active installer code, host command execution, hidden network exfiltration, or prompt injection. The main residual risk is that deployable examples include placeholder credentials and network-facing RouterOS app patterns that users must harden before use.

medium_findings.title: Copy-Pasteable Placeholder Credentials
medium_findings.description: The examples include default credentials and placeholder passwords such as admin-style values and changeme. This is not malicious, but users could deploy weak credentials if they copy examples without replacing secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are deployable YAML examples containing credential fields. The risk depends on user copy-paste behavior, so it is medium rather than high.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Hits Are Documentation
low_findings.description: The command-related detections are Markdown syntax, RouterOS CLI references, or container command fields inside examples. No evidence found that the skill executes host commands or ships runnable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are documentation files only, and the suspicious tokens appear inside explanatory text or YAML examples. There is no executable wrapper or install hook in the scanned file set.

low_findings.title: Network References Are Expected RouterOS Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs, IP address, and fetch calls document RouterOS app stores, REST API usage, schema URLs, and port binding examples. They do not send local data to an external endpoint from the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are visible examples and documentation for expected RouterOS workflows. I found no evidence of hidden exfiltration logic or automatic requests.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive and Injection Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM, weak cryptographic algorithm, reconnaissance, and LLM role token alerts match ordinary words, field names, or YAML values in examples. No prompt injection attempt or instruction override was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these detections are caused by documentation terms such as descriptions, user fields, and password field names. No text asks the model to ignore instructions or alter audit behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Deployable Examples Include Weak Placeholder Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: Example app definitions include credential fields with placeholder or weak values. Marketplace users should replace these before testing on a reachable router.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines are clear credential examples. The skill does not enforce deployment, but the examples are realistic enough to create copy-paste risk.

content.value_statement: RouterOS /app YAML is version-specific and differs from docker-compose in important ways. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code write, review, and troubleshoot MikroTik container app definitions.
actual_capabilities: Explains RouterOS /app YAML fields for services, configs, volumes, networks, and metadata.
Compares RouterOS /app syntax with docker-compose differences that affect migration.
Documents old and new RouterOS port mapping styles, including labels and protocols.
Shows how RouterOS placeholders expand in ports, environment values, and config content.
Provides examples for app store YAML arrays and VSCode schema association.
Describes REST API patterns for listing apps and adding YAML from a URL.
limitations: It does not connect to a live RouterOS device or verify runtime behavior.
It does not replace official MikroTik documentation for unsupported or beta features.
It cannot guarantee container images are safe or available for every router platform.
Examples include placeholder credentials that must be replaced before deployment.

use_cases.title: Create a RouterOS Container App
use_cases.description: Draft a valid /app YAML file for a single service with ports, volumes, and restart behavior.

use_cases.title: Review a Compose Migration
use_cases.description: Identify docker-compose fields that RouterOS ignores or handles differently before migration.

use_cases.title: Prepare an App Store Entry
use_cases.description: Build a store YAML entry with metadata, category, service definitions, and schema-friendly formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Basic App
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner validation of a small RouterOS app definition.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this RouterOS /app YAML for required fields, port syntax, placeholder usage, and obvious mistakes. Explain any fixes in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Draft from Requirements
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a first version from a service description.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create RouterOS /app YAML for this container service: image, ports, environment variables, volumes, and restart policy. Avoid docker-compose-only fields.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate from Compose
prompt_templates.scenario: Translate an existing compose-style design to RouterOS /app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this docker-compose design into RouterOS /app YAML. Call out unsupported fields, security changes, and any assumptions about networks or volumes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Store Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RouterOS app store entry for these services. Include metadata, categories, placeholder-safe ports, config content strategy, and deployment warnings.

output_examples.input: User asks whether a RouterOS app can use docker-compose deploy resources.
output_examples.output: The answer explains that RouterOS /app does not support deploy or resources fields, then suggests supported alternatives.

output_examples.input: User provides a port list with mixed syntax styles.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the mixed styles, recommends one RouterOS-compatible format, and explains protocol placement.

output_examples.input: User wants an app store entry for a monitoring service.
output_examples.output: The response outlines the app metadata, service definition, volume needs, category choice, and credential replacement steps.

best_practices: State the target RouterOS version before generating or validating /app YAML.
Replace all sample credentials and review exposed ports before deploying on a router.
Use strict schema URLs for CI checks and editor schema URLs for authoring assistance.
anti_patterns: Do not assume docker-compose files can be used unchanged on RouterOS.
Do not mix old and new port mapping styles inside the same app definition.
Do not publish app YAML that points to untrusted images, URLs, or default passwords.

faq.question: What RouterOS versions does this skill target?
faq.answer: It focuses on RouterOS 7.21 and later, with custom YAML creation described for 7.22 and later.

faq.question: Is RouterOS /app the same as docker-compose?
faq.answer: No. It has similar concepts, but many compose fields are missing or behave differently.

faq.question: Can this skill validate YAML against a live router?
faq.answer: No. It can reason from documented formats, but it does not connect to RouterOS.

faq.question: Does it support app store YAML files?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents store files as YAML arrays of app definitions.

faq.question: Why are port formats important?
faq.answer: RouterOS supports old slash protocol syntax and newer colon syntax. Mixing them can cause validation problems.

faq.question: Are the example passwords safe to use?
faq.answer: No. Treat all example credentials as placeholders and replace them before any deployment.
